description: During a desktop review, it was noticeable that the Paris and Rotterdam operational locations are not identified within the scope of the Business Continuity Arrangements – ENS-OPS-PRO-001, resulting in incomplete coverage of business continuity planning.

source_snapshot.Failure: During a desktop review, it was noticeable that the Paris and Rotterdam operational locations are not identified within the scope of the Business Continuity Arrangements – ENS-OPS-PRO-001, resulting in incomplete coverage of business continuity planning.

description:
As identified under Question 1, the fixed onshore locations in Paris and Rotterdam are not included within the scope of ENS-OPS-PRO-001 and, as a result, no Business Continuity Plans are in place for these locations.

In addition, the Business Continuity Plan for Blyth Base Facility (ENS-OPS-PLA-001) is not fully current or reflective of the present site arrangements. The plan requires review and update to ensure:
-	Section 5 contains an accurate and current list of worksite contacts,
-	Section 7 includes an up-to-date office floor plan, and the newly installed portacabins are incorporated within the site layout documentation
These gaps indicate that Business Continuity Plans are not consistently maintained in line with current operational locations and site configurations.

These gaps indicate that Business Continuity Plans are not consistently maintained in line with current operational locations and site configurations.

description:
ENS-OPS-PRO-001 (Sections 2 and 11.3) defines the requirement for the Managing Director to appoint a Business Continuity Team Leader (BCTL) and outlines the associated roles and responsibilities.

During a brief discussion with a management member, it was not clearly established who is currently designated to fulfil the BCTL role, nor how this appointment would be formally made in the event of a Business Continuity Event.

In addition, the procedure references the role of “Managing Director”, whereas the current organisational structure identifies the role as “CEO”, indicating that the documented governance structure has not been updated to reflect the present business configuration.

This indicates that the identification and communication of the BCTL role is not fully embedded or aligned with the current organisational structure.

This indicates that the identification and communication of the BCTL role is not fully embedded or aligned with the current organisational structure.

description:
ENS-OPS-PRO-001 (Section 10) defines the structure, roles, and responsibilities of the Crisis Management Team (CMT).

However, during interview sampling with a designated CMT member, the individual was unable to clearly provide their specific responsibilities or the procedural requirements associated with the activation and management of a Business Continuity Event.

This indicates that, although CMT roles are documented, there is insufficient evidence that responsibilities have been effectively communicated and embedded to ensure understanding across the team.

This presents a risk that, in the event of activation, CMT members may not perform their roles effectively or in accordance with the defined procedure.

description:
ENS-OPS-PRO-001 references ENS-HSEQ-FRM-034 (CMT Emergency Checklist) as a linked document to be used in the management of Crisis Management Team (CMT) events.

During document control review, although ENS-HSEQ-FRM-034 is listed on the document register, no controlled version of this form could be located within the server-based document management folders (HSEQS & Universal). In addition, a different document appears to exist under the same reference number, creating ambiguity regarding the correct controlled version. The other listed document is not recorded on the document register. 

No evidence was available to demonstrate that the referenced form is accessible, controlled, or available for use

description:
Although a Business Continuity exercise was conducted at Blyth in October/November 2024, no evidence was available to demonstrate that exercises have been conducted on an annual basis as required by Section 14 of ENS-OPS-PRO-001.

Furthermore, no evidence was available of exercises being conducted at Darlington or other applicable locations.

This indicates that the Business Continuity exercise requirement is not being consistently implemented or managed as a controlled annual programme.

This indicates that the Business Continuity exercise requirement is not being consistently implemented or managed as a controlled annual programme.

description:
In accordance with ENS-PROC-PRO-001, supplier approval activities are required to be completed using forms aligned to supplier criticality classification (FRM-002 for Criticality A, FRM-006 for Criticality B, and no formal approval form required for Criticality C suppliers).

A sample of suppliers was selected to verify implementation across differing criticality levels:

-	Criticality A – Osbit:
Objective evidence demonstrated that supplier approval had been completed using the correct form (FRM-002) in accordance with procedural requirements.

-	Criticality B – Durham Lifting:
No objective evidence could be provided to demonstrate that the required supplier approval process had been completed in line with procedural requirements.

-	Criticality B – JK Lifting:
Supplier onboarding evidence was available; however, approval was completed using FRM-002 rather than the required FRM-006, resulting in a deviation from the documented process.

description:
Supplier scope of supply is required to be documented, reviewed, and approved as part of the supplier approval process prior to inclusion on the Approved Vendor List (AVL).

A sample review was conducted using the same suppliers referenced in Item 1.2:

-	Osbit (Criticality A):
Objective evidence confirmed that the scope of supply had been defined, reviewed, and approved prior to AVL inclusion.

-	JK Lifting (Criticality B):
Evidence demonstrated that the supplier’s scope of supply had been documented and approved as part of the onboarding process.

-	Durham Lifting (Criticality B):
No objective evidence was available to demonstrate completion of supplier approval activities. Consequently, there was no evidence to confirm that the scope of supply had been formally reviewed and approved prior to addition to the AVL.

The absence of supplier approval evidence for Durham Lifting indicates that scope-of-supply review and approval requirements are not consistently evidenced prior to AVL inclusion. This finding links directly to the minor non-conformance raised in question 1.2.

description:
In accordance with ENS-PROC-PRO-003, the top suppliers on the AVL are required to undergo annual review using ENS-PROC-FRM-007 – Annual Supplier Details, to ensure ongoing assessment of supplier risk and performance.

Evidence reviewed included an Excel-based tracker identifying the top 10 suppliers, utilising defined scoring metrics to generate an overall risk rating. Sampling confirmed that FRM-007 had been completed for selected suppliers, including:
-	Site and Field Services — score 8/15 (Low Risk)
-	ROVOP — score 8/15 (Low Risk)
-	SMD — score 13/15 (High Risk)
However, review of completed FRM-007 records identified that forms had been completed by a Supply Chain Apprentice therefore, supplier annual evaluation records do not demonstrate appropriate review and authorisation by competent personnel, resulting in insufficient assurance that supplier risk scoring has been undertaken.

description:
The Approved Vendor List (AVL) is required to be maintained as a controlled record to ensure supplier status, approval information, and procurement decisions are based on current and accurate data.

During review, the AVL provided as objective evidence was identified as an outdated version, with no records for 2025 or 2026. No evidence was available to demonstrate that the latest controlled version had been downloaded, reviewed, or maintained. As a result, supplier information contained within the list did not reflect current status and completeness requirements.

description: It was noted that two fire extinguishers, located in Hot Works area and The Training Academy room, were be outside their required inspection period.
root_cause: There was heavy reliance on the external service provider to manage inspection intervals, but no internal process existed to verify completion, track due dates, or provide visibility of equipment status.
containment_action: Hazard cards have been raised to capture the issue internally, and a third-party fire extinguisher inspection has been arranged.
corrective_action:
1. Create a fire extinguisher log
2. Update/create the site emergency layout map highlighting the locations and types of fire extinguishers
3. Carry out montlhy verification checks on fire extinguishers and their locations

source_snapshot.Failure: It was noted that two fire extinguishers, located in Hot Works area and The Training Academy room, were be outside their required inspection period.
source_snapshot.Correction / Containment Action: Hazard cards have been raised to capture the issue internally, and a third-party fire extinguisher inspection has been arranged.
source_snapshot.Root Cause: There was heavy reliance on the external service provider to manage inspection intervals, but no internal process existed to verify completion, track due dates, or provide visibility of equipment status.
source_snapshot.Corrective Action:
1. Create a fire extinguisher log
2. Update/create the site emergency layout map highlighting the locations and types of fire extinguishers
3. Carry out montlhy verification checks on fire extinguishers and their locations

description: During a review of Inspection Certification, it was identified that the roller shutter door requires a yearly inspection, but the last recorded inspection was dated 12/24. This indicates that the required annual inspection has not been completed within the prescribed timeframe
root_cause: The statutory inspection requirement was not visible within routine site monitoring activities, as Workplace Inspection checks did not prompt verification of inspection status. This resulted in reliance on the external provider without an internal reminder mechanism.
containment_action: A third-party certification visit has been booked to carry out an inspection.
corrective_action:
Update ENS-HSEQ-FRM-044 Site Inspection Form to include a weekly visual check of roller shutter doors and a prompt to verify that statutory inspection dates remain in date.
This embeds monitoring within the existing Workplace Inspection process (ENS-HSEQ-PRO-023), ensuring inspection status is routinely reviewed and future renewals cannot be overlooked.

This embeds monitoring within the existing Workplace Inspection process (ENS-HSEQ-PRO-023), ensuring inspection status is routinely reviewed and future renewals cannot be overlooked.

description: During a review of the waste management documentation, it was identified that requirements for managing Food Waste was not detailed within the formal Procedure, despite being clearly defined and recorded within the Compliance Register.
root_cause: Procedural updates had not been considered until the time of the audit, as relevant information was highlighted in the compliance register.
containment_action:
The Waste Management Procedure ENS-HSEQ-PRO-016  was reviewed during the audit and updated to include specific controls for segregation, storage, and disposal of food waste in accordance with identified compliance obligations.
The revised procedure was issued under document control and communicated to relevant personnel.
corrective_action: HSEQ will now complete a brief documented check when Compliance Register entries are updated to confirm that any referenced procedures already reflect the requirement, or that an update has been made where necessary.

source_snapshot.Corrective Action: HSEQ will now complete a brief documented check when Compliance Register entries are updated to confirm that any referenced procedures already reflect the requirement, or that an update has been made where necessary.

description:
Failure to Consistently Apply Management of Change Process to Business Changes.

Procedure ENS-HSEQ-PRO-009 requires that significant organisational and business changes be formally identified, risk assessed, and controlled through the MOC process.

The audit identified multiple examples of significant business changes occurring without a formal MOC being raised, including but not limited to:
•	Establishment of additional Enshore office locations (Paris and Rotterdam)
•	Changes to organisational structure following acquisition of Enshore by Al Gihaz
•	Creation of new corporate entities (Enshore Subsea Rapidscan Limited; Enshore Subsea Assets Limited)
•	Significant leadership transitions (creation of C-Suite structure).

These changes represent material organisational risk and must be controlled.  Failure to apply the MOC process to these changes represents a breakdown in organisational adherence to the procedure and undermines the purpose of the Management of Change framework.

source_snapshot.Failure:

Incomplete MOC Documentation

Several open MOC records contain incomplete sections including:
•	Missing implementation schedules
•	Uncompleted action plans
•	Missing approvals and signatures.

Incomplete documentation reduces traceability and limits the ability to demonstrate that risks have been adequately assessed and controlled.

description:
MOC Process Ownership

In practice, the MOC process is largely administered by the Quality function rather than initiated by operational departments.

Whilst the Quality Manager is responsible for maintaining the procedure and auditing compliance, the procedure clearly assigns responsibility for initiating MOCs to the relevant Director or Head of Department.

Greater ownership by departmental leadership is required to ensure the process functions as intended.

Whilst the Quality Manager is responsible for maintaining the procedure and auditing compliance, the procedure clearly assigns responsibility for initiating MOCs to the relevant Director or Head of Department.

Greater ownership by departmental leadership is required to ensure the process functions as intended.

description:
Strategic Change Governance

Enshore has undergone several major strategic and structural changes over the last 18 months, including leadership restructuring and expansion into additional geographic locations.

These changes represent exactly the type of organisational risk that the MOC process is designed to control.

The lack of consistent MOC application to these changes indicates that the process is not yet embedded within strategic decision-making processes.

description:
Documentation reviewed confirmed that job descriptions are established and maintained, including defined roles across the organisation (e.g. Quality Manager, Supervisor). The document reviewed was “TONE JD”, Revision 5, dated 17 October 2025.

The job descriptions provide an overview of responsibilities and role expectations, supporting the identification of competency requirements within the organisation.

However, it was noted that the organisational chart embedded within the job description document is not aligned with the latest organisational structure, as a more recent version of the organisational chart (January 2026) was presented during the audit.

However, it was noted that the organisational chart embedded within the job description document is not aligned with the latest organisational structure, as a more recent version of the organisational chart (January 2026) was presented during the audit.

description:
Evidence reviewed confirmed that inspection and measurement equipment is subject to calibration through an external provider, with records maintained within a centralised database system accessible to TrackOne.

The system provides visibility of calibration certificates and defined calibration intervals, supporting the control of equipment fitness for purpose. Sampled equipment during the audit included:
•	Torque Wrench – ID: T STR V0017 / Certificate: 2604348FRE1
•	Micrometer – ID: T.STR.C0007 / Certificate: 2604198DRE6
Calibration certificates for the sampled equipment were available and in date, demonstrating that equipment calibration is being effectively managed.

However, it was noted that traceability of inspection and measurement equipment during the assembly process is not recorded. For example, there is no documented linkage between specific tools (e.g. torque wrenches) and the components or assemblies on which they were used.

source_snapshot.Failure:

However, it was noted that traceability of inspection and measurement equipment during the assembly process is not recorded. For example, there is no documented linkage between specific tools (e.g. torque wrenches) and the components or assemblies on which they were used.

description:
During the shopfloor inspection, lifting slings were observed with blue colour-coded tags, indicating that they had been inspected and deemed fit for use.

However, multiple slings of the same type and size (green slings) were also observed without the required colour-coded tags, indicating inconsistency in the application of the colour coding system.

Discussions with the HSE Manager confirmed that the untagged slings were considered “new” and therefore acceptable for use. However, no documented procedure, instruction, or defined control was available to support the use of lifting equipment without an applied colour code, regardless of condition.

It was further confirmed that:
•	Colour coding is carried out quarterly by an external provider
•	The Plant Manager is responsible for reapplying tags where required or for newly introduced equipment

Despite this, no evidence was available to demonstrate:
•	A defined process for controlling newly purchased lifting equipment prior to use
•	A requirement for tagging prior to use
•	Clear visual communication of the current colour code system on the shopfloor

The inconsistent application of lifting equipment colour coding, combined with the absence of a documented control process for new or replacement equipment, creates a risk that unverified lifting equipment may be used.

This indicates that controls relating to the identification and verification of lifting equipment are not fully defined or consistently implemented.

description:
Review of waste transfer documentation identified that Category 3 catering waste is subcontracted by REMONDIS to Warrens Group. While the documentation confirms the use of a third-party contractor, no supporting evidence was available within the sample to demonstrate that the downstream contractor has been formally approved, verified, or assessed.

As Category 3 catering waste is classified as an Animal By-Product and subject to additional regulatory controls, this presents a potential gap in demonstrating full Duty of Care across the waste chain. Further information is required to confirm that appropriate controls, licences, and treatment routes are in place.

As Category 3 catering waste is classified as an Animal By-Product and subject to additional regulatory controls, this presents a potential gap in demonstrating full Duty of Care across the waste chain. Further information is required to confirm that appropriate controls, licences, and treatment routes are in place.

description: Supplier onboarding documentation (ENS-PROC-FRM-006) indicates scope limited to engineering consultancy services. Current scope includes subcontract crane modification works. Full onboarding reassessment may be required – Enshore to investigate.
root_cause: Project Management Team not liaising directly with Supply Chain Team.
containment_action: Full ENS-PROC-FRM-002 sent to SDJ for population. Completed form received back and filed: "Z:\Universal\Supply Chain\Procurement\Suppliers\Supplier Onboarding\Onboarding Forms - New Suppliers\SDJ\ENS-PROC-FRM-002 Vendor Approval Submission Form - SDJ.pdf"
corrective_action: Head of Assets appointed to liaise between PM and Supply Chain Teams.

description:
Over-reliance on Managing Director for:
•	Quality 
•	HSE 
•	Auditing 
•	Procurement

HSE responsibilities sit solely with Managing Director; no formal qualifications evidenced and there are a lack of deputy roles (fire warden), creating single-point dependency. This would have been considered a non conformance against ISO 9001 cl. 7.2 / 8.1 (in terms of business continuity / emergency risk), however it is noted that SDJ are tenants in a larger, managed building which reduces the risk of single point dependencies.  If SDJ were to move to an unmanaged premises, this weakness should be addressed.

Additionally: Competency matrix shows internal audit responsibility held by Managing Director only, with no wider organisational capability.
root_cause: To follow up during next audit
containment_action: To follow up during next audit
corrective_action: To follow up during next audit

description:
Document reference: 105124-R13
Revision: 13
Date: 13-MAR-2026

Defined organisational structure and competency matrix in place; however, gaps identified in purchasing, complaints handling and release readiness.  Whilst not currently a non conformance as resource in place is deemed sufficient for the size of the organization, these gaps should be addressed if SDJ scale up in the future.

description:
Changes to design / project scope / methodologies are controlled and  captured in project records; there is no clear mechanism for feeding back changes into QMS updates where the changes are permanent. 

Whilst there is no explicit non conformance noted against ISO 9001:2015 cl. 10.3 (Continuous improvement), a means to incorporate ‘lessons learned’ at a management system level is recommended.
root_cause: To follow up during next audit
containment_action: To follow up during next audit
corrective_action: To follow up during next audit
